entries.transcript: The miracle of human innovation and cooperation and my own creativity when supported by the resourcefulness of this collective endeavor catapulted me into superstardom. I started to feel that I had arrived. And genuinely by the time I was 40 I was really really flying. I was all over the place, you know, I I'd done 50 films by then and 200 songs. And I'd been knighted by the Malaysians. I'd been given the highest civil honor by the French government, the title of which for the life of me.

I didn't give up then. I even tried to reconstruct my identity on the social media like everyone else does. I thought if I put on philosophical tweets out there, people will think I'm with it. But some of the responses I got for those tweets were extremely confusing acronyms which I didn't understand, you know, R O F L, L O L. Adidas, Adidas somebody wrote back to one of my more thought-provoking tweets. And I was wondering why would you name a sneaker, I mean why would you write back a name of a sneaker to me. And I asked my 16-year-old daughter and she enlightened me. Adidas

I'm sure each one of you present over here has either falls into any one of these categories or all.

I was born in Bahrain, though both my parents are from Ujjain.So, my father was an aircraft maintenance engineer in Bahrain and that's why I was born there. Now, you know, I was the youngest of six siblings. So anybody who's the youngest over here in their family, they would resonate with me when I say that hum apne badon ki galtiyon se bhi seekhte hain aur unke experience se bhi seekhte hain. So you know how it happens? Like if my elder sister is getting scolded, scolded for not finishing her food, I would gut down mine because I know

And what I learnt over there is, ke aap kuch bhi kar lo, doctor ban jao, engineer ban jao, in the end you have to deal with people. And many a times difficult people. In the end you might be put into a nasty situation and how you come out, that is what is showing your efficiency. I would like to give you an example of an efficient communication that happened with me. So while I was doing my telemarketing job, you know how it is, they give you a manual and you

a probable set of questions that the customer might ask. So after 45 days of no leads, finally one gentleman agreed and he said that yes, I have a requirement of home loan. I was not not even 19. I was very young at that time. And I was so happy ke haan yaar finally koi lead mila. But after a few minutes, that gentleman started asking me for my mobile number. Now here I was in a very bad situation for two reasons. Number one, mere paas

And I I had the opportunity to learn from Mr. I. C. Dhingra. The 11th, 12th commerce students must be knowing him. Aaj bhi unki books chalti hain NCERT mein. So kabhi kabhi hota hai na ki gyan se bada guru ho jata hai. The same thing happened with me and I decided to do my PhD in economics. Now everything was going doing great. I was working in the National Stock Exchange and I I went into training. I went into training, I went into communication and after that I

Today when I start, I want to talk starting, I mean, yeah, I want to start talking about my failures first because in life if there's anything that I've learned then it is through my failures. So there are two important failures in my life. So the first one was when I was in school, I was too young to process a failure then. Yeah, but unfortunately, but fortunately it happened. I was in fourth standard and

And we'd begin with the prayer. And after that I had to announce pledge, then we'd say the pledge. And after that, we had all the activities like thought for the day, speech and everything and finally, I had to say national anthem, then we'd proceed with the national anthem. So that was the process like every other assembly and I was very confident about it because I'd been practicing that for a week or so. So I thought okay fine, this is very easy, I can manage it. I started

Unfortunately, the whole school started laughing at me. And I was so, so, so scared. Like I was so embarrassed. I didn't expect that to be happening to me, that to I was I was too young to process that. That was the main problem. And I just stood there froze, didn't know what to do. My teacher came to the rescue. She took the mic, she proceeded with the assembly. And yeah, I made a mess of my first class assembly and

Like how bad can that be? Whenever I go to a washroom, whenever I go somewhere, they just address me as, isn't that the National Anthem girl? So I'd be like, oh my god, again. I didn't even want to go to school because I'd hear this National Anthem girl who would want to be reminded of their embarrassments every single day. So I somehow wanted to change people's perspective about me. So what I did is I thought I'll study, I'll study hard so that

And I have to admit that it was kind of working because every time I accomplish something by studying, my teachers, you know, they'd appreciate me a lot. I'd be their pets and other students, they thought that if they hung out with me, their grades would improve. So I just felt good about myself. I was starting to enjoy that and I was like a horse with the blinders on. All I could see was my books.

But they were not happy. I did realize that I was lacking in something. So I wanted to make my mother very happy. So what I would do is I would just join my group dances that was happening during the youth festival, you know, you have those youth festivals every year where you can go and take part. So I would go and dance in the group, you know, in one corner so that people don't notice me. I just wanted to be part of the, you know, dancing thing. I just wanted to make my mother happy. So every

A negative thought comes to your head, just counter it with a positive one. Because I'll tell you what, when you have positive beliefs in your head, when you repeat positive thoughts, what happens in your brain is new neural pathways are created to support that belief. And that is how the transformation scientifically occurs. And then you transform yourself, you change the perspective that

So, I knew nothing about acting. Okay, initially, I was doing engineering and I used to see these big Bollywood stars on screen. They all performed really well. So I thought it was easy. They just did it so effortlessly. They faked it so well that it made me think that it was very easy. And I told my grandmother that I want to go for an audition and I want to try. So my grandmother, she was concerned.
So I thought it would help me, but unfortunately it doesn't work like that. I realized it the hard way. So I started with my audition, I was like, uh,

And I didn't know what to do and at the end I was like, sir, I think I've already made a mess of my biryani, so I was laughing. But I actually was very embarrassed. So the director was like, no, no, no, you were doing fine. Like I knew that he was consoling me. So then my grandmother was still in that shock, like she she couldn't look at me at all. She didn't want to see me, I think. So the director said, it's fine, you've done you've done good. Now your resume says that

So I was like, okay sir, I didn't want to dance. I just wanted to run from there, but just because he said that, I was like, okay, okay sir, I'll do a dance performance. And they put the music and I started dancing. That girl who used to do the group dances in some corner, no, that just came to me and I started dancing with the music. I didn't even realize what was going on in front of me. I was involved in the music and I think the director was happy with the dance.

performance probably I don't know but he his face just lit up after the performance he was like telling me you did well but I knew that I didn't do the acting part very well which was the most important part so I was very embarrassed and my grandmother still couldn't look at my face okay there's another thing and the director said we'll give you a call in two days so I was I was very sure that this is not gonna happen so I was like okay sum see you then I knew I'm not gonna see him again so I just went back home and my

One person who just came to me, he knew that I was helpless and embarrassed. He did realize my situation. So he came to me and he said, Amina, you need to observe yourself. Only do what you really feel in a certain situation. When somebody gives you a topic, only if you would really do that in your real life, incorporate it in your performance, otherwise don't do it. So, it is at that point that I realized that

I'd been overacting all this while. I was not doing things that I would actually do in my real life. I was just making up things and maybe, you know, this is how people do it and I just I was just doing something. But that's not how acting works. So, that is how I did realize the importance of observation And that really did help me in my career as an actor because the first step was a big failure and from then

Okay, then you're not so representative because actually we have half the Indian population pretty much under 25. We are an amazingly young country. In fact, if you just take the age group from 10 to 19, there are 226 million Indians poised in other words to enter higher education, going through school and ready for higher education. Now this is amazing because it's happening at a time when the rest of the world is aging. Right, if you look at the average age in India today, it's 28.

Of course, don't ask about the gap. since we heard about gaps between the average age of the Indian person and of the Indian cabinet. I think we hold the world record for that. But that's that's another matter. That's another another TED talk, right? But what you've got with this average age is a time when the rest of the world is changing. So by 2020, the average age in Japan's going to be 47. In China it's going to be heading well past 40,Europe 46, the United States, youthful US, also 40, and

in terms of addressing things like the gender gap. That's a gap that despite what Mr. well what the Earlier speakers said we don't want to embrace, right? It's a gap we must, must overcome. Right now women's literacy is 66%, better than the 8.9, but still means that, you know, one out of every three Indian women still can't read and write. We have to overcome that. We need to catch the ones who've been left out of the net. Adult literacy. Huge challenge. I went off to a village in Tamil Nadu not far from Kanjivaram

As our lives and everything around us seems to be moving, seems to be like somebody played a tape recorder, pressed the fast forward button and left it that way. It's all moving at such a rapid pace, right? So it is only fair to us to pause, breathe, and take a moment to think about the past. The good we faced, the bad we experienced, but mind you, when you move forward in life, the only baggage that you would want to carry forward should be the good.

be the good  Because it would be an unpleasant case otherwise As I sat down to think about how to brainstorm and compile everything that I wanted to talk about today, there was one moment, it was more of a revelation, when I realized if one 23 year old had so many things to talk about, so much to say, imagine the kind of life experiences and stories you would get to hear from people around the world. exciting to think about it. Let me take you through mine now So I was born and raised in the beautiful Chennai city, but my roots go back to a small village in called Edur in Iritty, Kerala. Though I was raised, schooled, everything in Tamil Nadu, my connect with my homeland Kerala is very strong. In fact, it's as strong as my love for Kappa, fish curry, pazhampuri and chaya is. There are Malayalis here, you

But I think I am privileged to say I believe that I am equally a Telugu Ammai now as much as I am a Malayali and a Tamilian. And this because this cultural integration is what makes me call anywhere home. And I believe this cultural integration is necessary for us to grow better. Rolling back a couple of years, now we're getting into the the the topic. I'm going

to share with you a couple of my life experiences, what I went through and whatever I am today is because of all these experiences I I had. So I would like to share it with all of you. So rolling back a couple of years, the word school. The word school resonated with an album to me. Like a photo album of Polaroids flashing in front of my eyes, I have very vivid memories of what school meant. I faced the camera first when I was eight years old

and since then, believe it or not, I have been to school sometimes, visited school, by visit I mean, literally just visited school three or four times, three or four days a month. Honestly, that's how much I've been there. There are I I think definitely my teachers must have seen chief guests repeat themselves to events more than I've actually been in school. But going to work, acting, you know, on longish

schedules and then coming back to sitting in class, being attentive, completing class works, being on par with where the rest of the class was, was very taxing. Sometimes I would also want to give up removing all the makeup before I fly to bed. But before I could think about it or realize, you know, the the the struggle with all this, This had already become my life. So, as a kid,

I remember, I wanted to take a moment to decide on few things before I proceeded forward. I wanted to draw a line between work and study. That's what I wanted to do. It sounds like a very, very simple idea, but trust me when I say this, it required a battalion, a battalion of people to execute. I needed my teachers, my principal, my friends from workplace, my friends from school, my producers, my directors

all my technicians and my family more than anything. And today when I look back and think about it, I have been more than blessed. I feel like I've been blessed more than required to have had them all. In fact, my school was so supportive, my principal, Brother George, was so supportive of me going to act that the three or four days that I would go to go to school, he would look at me and be like, oh Nivetha, you're here. When are you going back to work? I was like, let me

This was extremely taxing, it was. But while also listening to script readings and there were a lot of narrations happening, which I would okay at one point so that I can work on those films when I had my semester vacation. So this is how my pattern in college life was. All this was happening, but the real struggle was inside me.I was constantly at war with my mind.

enhancing digital theater sound, visual effects, all of those to tell better and better stories. But what happened then? It all changed. It all changed how? It changed with the medium of of the digital world. We had something called a film laboratory where film used to be shot, processed, and then ultimately made ready with multiple copies of prints for all of you all to watch in the cinemas. With the rise of digital, that went away, so the infrastructure was not the same anymore.

walk in to this great world where the movie is being made. Yes, all this is happening today. Now who makes all these things? Is it artists? No. We just are tools to tell these stories. But it is people with technology, people who understand innovation. So that really was the change. When I speak at events like these, the first thing that someone asked me yesterday was, why are you speaking at TED? It's not a paid event, you're an actor. So the only reason I'm here is

to tell all of you guys that there is a space out here, there is an opportunity. And why would we able to do it? Because change comes with a lot of hard decisions to make. When film moved away, my mother used to run the film laboratory. And everything became digital, nobody was shooting on film anymore. We had to shut the film laboratory, an institution that was there for many, many years. We had to let very, very close personnel go away. And that's really heartbreaking for all of us

propagate our culture, we will put it up there on this silver screen for the world to see. and it was not just him. It was the producers of that film. ask you guys by the end of all of this is, If you can empower us with innovation, if you can create better ways for us to tell stories, if you can help us with your great business practices that you have from across various business platforms, come in here and let's tell stories together.
